Maladaptive Strategies.value: I try to erase the memory of the event
I drift off into a world of my own

Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
Nothing good can happen to me anymore
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

vignette:
Mr. Abebe is a 71-year-old single man from East Africa who presents with significant distress following his exposure to a terrorist attack in his home country several years ago. His life is now dominated by a constant state of vigilance. The sight of a police officer’s uniform, the sound of a car backfiring, or even an accidental glimpse of blood on television can instantly transport him back to the chaos of that day. In these moments, the world falls away, replaced by the overwhelming sensory reality of the past; he feels the heat and hears the screams as if he were there again, his heart pounding violently in his chest.

These intrusive experiences are not vague impressions but are instead experienced with agonizing clarity. His memories of the event are like a vividly detailed film that plays on a relentless loop in his mind, each frame a sharp, photographic image he cannot escape. The uninvited return of these memories reinforces his deepest fears: that he is fundamentally broken, that he can no longer trust his own judgment, and that a future of emotional numbness is his only prospect.

Driven by the intensity of these physical reactions and the painful clarity of his recollections, Mr. Abebe has developed several entrenched coping rituals. He frequently checks his reflection in mirrors, a compulsive reassurance-seeking behavior. He consciously tries to force the memories from his mind, willing them to be erased. When the internal pressure becomes too great, he simply disconnects, drifting off into a private, internal world of fantasy. His pervasive belief that nothing good can happen to him anymore means that ordinary outings feel pointless and fraught with potential triggers, which he then actively works to avoid.

Consequently, Mr. Abebe lives in a state of profound isolation. His difficulty concentrating makes sustained conversation or simple tasks a struggle. The cycle is self-perpetuating: a triggering sight or sound sparks both a visceral threat response and a flood of precise, unwanted memories. These, in turn, feed his negative appraisals of himself and his future, which further heighten his sense of threat and compel more avoidance and maladaptive coping. He is trapped in a closed system where the past is never truly past, and the strategies he uses to survive only serve to maintain his prison.
